title: Why the return of anger is actually a sign of progress
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

 For years, my anger scared me. It felt like this hot, dangerous thing I had to keep locked away. If I let it out,...
metaDescription:
I thought I was broken. Then I got angry. 

 For years, my anger scared me. It felt like this hot, dangerous thing I had to keep locked away. If I let it...
htmlContent:
I thought I was broken. Then I got angry.

For years, my anger scared me. It felt like this hot, dangerous thing I had to keep locked away. If I let it out, I was sure I would shatter everything. My relationships. My carefully constructed calm. My identity as a "good person." So I swallowed it. I smiled when I wanted to scream. I said "I'm fine" when I was drowning. I became an expert at dissociation, at leaving my body, at pretending the rage wasn't there.

Here's the thing. That anger was never the enemy. It was a signal. A message from a part of me that had been silenced for so long. And when it finally came back, after years of numbness and people-pleasing, I thought I was regressing. I thought I had failed at healing.

I hadn't. I was finally making progress.

Let me explain.

The frozen child and the volcano

When you grow up in an environment where your needs aren't safe to express, you learn to shut down. Your nervous system, as Stephen Porges taught us with his Polyvagal Theory, defaults to the dorsal vagal state. That's the freeze response. The collapse. The "I'll just go numb and wait for this to be over" mode. It's a survival strategy, and it works. For a while.

But here's what no one tells you. That frozen state isn't peace. It's not healing. It's a tomb. And inside that tomb, there's a volcano waiting.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets stuck in the body. The energy of the fight response, the anger that couldn't be expressed in the moment, it doesn't just disappear. It gets trapped. It lives in your shoulders, your jaw, your gut. It shows up as chronic tension, migraines, digestive issues. It whispers in your ear at 3 AM.

So when that anger starts to surface, it feels like a betrayal. You think, "I've been working so hard. Why am I still so angry?" But the truth is simpler and harder. That anger has always been there. You just finally feel safe enough to let it out.

Think about it. You can't feel anger if you're dissociated. You can't feel anger if you're in full freeze. Anger is a ventral vagal emotion, according to Porges. It requires a certain level of safety and connection to the body. It's a sign that your nervous system is coming back online. That you're thawing.

That's not regression. That's resurrection.

Anger as a boundary, not a weapon

One of the most confusing things about complex trauma recovery is learning to distinguish between healthy anger and the destructive kind. For many of us, the only model we had for anger was abuse. So when we feel that heat rising, we panic. We assume we're about to become the people who hurt us.

But that's not how it works. A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It gently reminds us that our responses are adaptations, not character flaws. Your anger is not a sign of something wrong with you. It's a sign that something was wrong with what happened to you.

Healthy anger is a boundary. It's the part of you that says, "No. This is not okay. I deserve better." It's the energy that helps you walk away from toxic relationships, set limits with family members, and stop abandoning yourself to keep the peace. It's the fire that burns away the old patterns of compliance and silence.

I remember the first time I got angry in therapy. I was talking about something my parent had done, and I felt this surge of heat in my chest. My therapist noticed. She said, "What's happening right now?" And I started crying, ashamed. "I'm so angry," I said. "I thought I was past this."

She smiled. Not a condescending smile. A knowing one. "That's not a problem," she said. "That's a sign that you're finally here."

Wild, right? The thing I thought was proof of my failure was actually proof of my presence.

The return of the exiled self

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), talks about how we exile parts of ourselves to survive. The angry part, the vulnerable part, the needy part, they all get locked away in the basement of our psyche. We become fragmented. We lose access to our full humanity.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. Walker explains how the "fight" type of trauma response is often the most stigmatized, even within the healing community. We're taught to be gentle, to be soft, to forgive. And those things have their place. But not before we've honored the anger.

The return of anger is the return of an exiled part. It's the part that was told, "You don't get to be mad. You don't get to have needs. You don't get to take up space." And now, finally, that part is knocking on the door. It's saying, "I'm here. I was always here. And I have something to say."

You don't have to act on every angry impulse. That's where the work comes in. But you do have to listen. You have to let that part speak. You have to give it room to breathe.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Dr. Arielle Schwartz. It offers exercises for tracking the body's signals, including anger, and learning to respond with curiosity instead of shame. It's not about getting rid of the anger. It's about understanding what it's trying to tell you.

What your anger wants you to know

So if you're reading this and you're feeling that familiar heat, that tightness in your jaw, that urge to punch something, I want you to pause. Stay with me here. Don't push it away. Don't judge it. Just notice it.

Your anger is not a monster. It's a messenger. And it's probably saying one of these things:

You were treated unfairly. You deserved better. Your boundaries were violated. Your needs were dismissed. Your voice was silenced. And it's not okay.

That's all. That's the message. It's not complicated. It's just been waiting a long time to be heard.

The wellness industry has sold us a lie. It's told us that healing means being calm, being peaceful, being "positive" all the time. But that's not healing. That's spiritual bypassing. That's putting a fresh coat of paint on a rotting foundation. A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It reminds us that there are no bad parts, only parts that have been burdened. Your anger is not bad. It's burdened.

Let it speak. Let it move. Let it be witnessed.

You don't have to act on it. You don't have to hurt anyone, including yourself. But you do have to feel it. You have to let it flow through your body, like a wave, without gripping it or pushing it away. That's how it gets released. That's how it transforms from a weapon into a compass.

Here's the hard truth. The return of anger is not the end of the healing journey. It's the beginning of the real work. It means you're no longer hiding. It means you're no longer pretending. It means you're finally willing to feel what you've been carrying alone for so long.

And that takes courage. More courage than staying numb ever did.

So if you're angry, good. Let that be a sign. A sign that you're coming back to life. A sign that your nervous system trusts you enough to feel. A sign that the exiled part of you is finally being welcomed home.

Sit with that. Let it land. And know that you're not broken. You're thawing.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's essential guide for C-PTSD survivors
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook by Dr. Arielle Schwartz
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems
